data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was a demographic and social revolution driven by a 'barbarian' labor revolt within the palace economies, not an external invasion.", "mechanism": "The palace redistributive system relied on a large, disenfranchised class of corvée laborers, captives, and semi-free peasants (the 'Apiru, Shardana, and other groups mentioned in texts). The intensification of the palace system in the 13th century BC led to increased extraction. A pandemic or series of poor harvests (the trigger) disproportionately affected the elite due to their reliance on stored, rat-infested grain, while simultaneously increasing labor demands on the surviving underclass. This sparked a coordinated, multi-regional uprising of these marginalized groups who burned the palaces, not as external 'Sea Peoples' invaders, but as internal rebels who then formed the post-collapse 'Sea Peoples' societies.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "The destruction layers at all major sites show a uniform pattern of sudden, violent conflagration with no evidence of preceding internal unrest or selective targeting of elite storage areas.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian mortuary texts and temple reliefs at Medinet Habu explicitly describe the Sea Peoples as foreign invaders from islands and the north, with women and children in ox-carts, not as internal rebels.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "This conjecture inverts the standard 'invasion' narrative. It explains the simultaneity (shared social structures), the ferocity of destruction (class hatred), and Egypt's survival (Ramesses III crushed a genuine external migration of the already-successful rebels, not the initial uprising). It's a high-risk, high-reward theory that reframes the iconic Medinet Habu reliefs as propaganda against a social revolution."}
